steps.title: Identify what evidence would support the student's assertion
steps.content: The student claims that beyond universal factors common to all Western countries, country-specific factors must have influenced how marrying age changed. To support this claim, the data must show that the rate of change differed between countries — not just that baseline ages differed.

steps.title: Match the divergent pattern to the choices
steps.content: Between 1970 and 2000, men in England and Wales rose from roughly 27 to 34 years (approximately 7-year increase), while US men rose from roughly 23 to 26 years (approximately 3-year increase). Choice A directly captures this divergence: the sharp rise in E&W versus the modest rise in the US demonstrates that the same 30-year period produced different outcomes in different countries. This pattern of divergent change—not just different absolute levels—supports the student's claim that country-specific factors shaped distinct trajectories. Choices B and D describe static baseline differences between countries, which is consistent with universal factors; choice C contains false data. That matches choice A — A is correct.
